title: Classic shortbread
subtitle: Scottish butter biscuit using the three-parts flour to two-parts butter ratio
excerpt: Shortbread is made from three parts flour, two parts butter, and one part sugar by weight. No egg. No leavening. The biscuit depends entirely on the quality of the butter and the restraint of the baker: overworking the dough activates the gluten and makes it tough, not short. The finished biscuit should snap cleanly and melt almost immediately on the tongue.
sourceNotes: Shortbread has been documented as a Scottish speciality since at least the 16th century, with early recipes appearing under the name short bread in Scottish manuscript cookbooks. Mrs McLintock, Receipts for Cookery and Pastry-Work (1736), one of the earliest Scottish cookbooks, gives a shortbread recipe with butter, flour, and sugar. The three-to-two-to-one ratio (flour, butter, sugar) became the standard Scottish formula and is the basis of the recipe here. Rice flour added in small amounts gives a finer, more crumbly texture that some bakers prefer; it is optional.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked shortbread in a single layer for up to 3 months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shortbread keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 140°C. The low temperature is deliberate: shortbread should not colour deeply. Pale gold at the edges is correct. Any browning on the surface means the oven is too hot.

recipeTools.notes: two baking trays, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: The classic Scottish shortbread formula: three parts plain flour to two parts butter to one part caster sugar by weight. This ratio gives a biscuit that is short (the butter coats the flour particles and inhibits gluten development), crumbly, and rich. Increasing the butter proportion makes the biscuit more melt-in-the-mouth but harder to handle. Replacing a quarter of the plain flour with rice flour gives a finer, sandier texture without altering the ratio.

content.text: . Mix the dough by hand rather than in a machine so you can feel when the butter has been worked enough: the dough should just come together into a cohesive mass that is neither crumbling nor glossy. Stop at that point. If the dough warms up and becomes greasy, wrap it and chill for 15 minutes before rolling.

attrs.prepNote: plus extra to dust the finished biscuits
optional; replace with extra plain flour if not using

content.text: Beat the softened butter and caster sugar together until just combined, not fluffy. Sift in the flour (and rice flour if using) and work the dough together with your hands until it just comes together into a smooth ball. Do not overwork. Wrap in clingfilm and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 160°C conventional (140°C fan). Roll the dough out on a lightly floured surface to 1 cm thickness. Cut into fingers approximately 8 × 3 cm. Prick each finger twice with a fork. Arrange on lined baking trays and bake for 22 to 25 minutes until pale gold at the edges. They will look underdone on top; that is correct. Dust with caster sugar immediately on leaving the oven. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack; they firm as they cool.
